prompt: The Dwaine fit is sales-hat = -68.86 + 1.45 targtpop + 9.37 dispoinc. Which reading of the 9.37 coefficient on disposable income is correct?
options: Raise a city's income by one unit and its sales rise 9.37, whatever else moves.
Among cities with the SAME target population, one with 1 unit more disposable income is predicted to sell about 9.37 thousand dollars more.
Disposable income explains 9.37 percent of the variation in sales.
The simple regression of sales on income alone has slope 9.37.
why: A multiple regression coefficient is a partial slope (Section 8.1): the change in mean sales per one-unit rise in income with the other predictor, target population, held fixed. It is not a causal promise about intervening, not a percent of variation, and not the simple-regression slope, which is the much steeper 31.17.

prompt: The income coefficient falls from 31.17 to 9.37 when target population joins the model. One sentence about this is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: The simple slope of sales on disposable income alone is 31.17.
Adding target population changes the income coefficient to 9.37.
The coefficient shrank because income and population are positively correlated (r = 0.78).
The shrinkage proves the original 31.17 slope was computed incorrectly.

why: Nothing was miscomputed: a coefficient belongs to a predictor and the company it keeps (Section 8.2). The question changed, not the arithmetic. The simple slope 31.17 lets population ride along with income; the partial slope 9.37 holds population fixed, and the two differ precisely because the predictors are correlated.

prompt: Body-fat sequential ANOVA (order triceps, thigh, midarm): triceps 352.27, thigh 33.17, midarm 11.55, Residuals 98.40. What is the 33.17 on the thigh row?
options: The variation thigh explains on its own, ignoring triceps.
SSR(thigh | triceps): the extra variation thigh removes AFTER triceps is already in the model.
The residual sum of squares left after all three predictors.
The total sum of squares of body fat.
why: A sequential (Type I) table gives each predictor the extra sum of squares it earns given the ones listed before it (Section 8.3). Here 33.17 = SSE(triceps) - SSE(triceps, thigh) = 143.12 - 109.95. Because triceps and thigh overlap, thigh entering second gets only its own crescent, far less than the 381.97 it would claim entering first.

prompt: Order the steps of the general linear test for dropping a set of coefficients.
steps: Fit the reduced model (the dropped terms forced to zero), record SSE(R)
Fit the full model (the terms kept), record SSE(F)
Form the extra sum of squares SSE(R) - SSE(F)
Divide by q, the number of coefficients set to zero
Divide by MSE(F), then compare F* to F(q, n - pF)
why: The general linear test scores the extra sum of squares the full model explains, per dropped coefficient, against the full model's noise estimate MSE(F) (Section 8.4). The result F* = ([SSE(R) - SSE(F)]/q) / (SSE(F)/(n - pF)) follows F(q, n - pF) when the reduced model is true, and a large F* is evidence the dropped terms are not all zero.

prompt: Added-variable plot for income in the Dwaine model. Match each piece to what it is.

pairs.meaning: how cleanly income's unique part predicts sales

why: An added-variable plot purges the other predictors from BOTH axes, then plots the leftovers (Section 8.5). By the Frisch-Waugh result its least-squares slope equals the multiple regression coefficient exactly, which is why the slope prints as 9.37. Cleaning only one axis breaks that equality and yields an uninterpretable hybrid slope.

prompt: Each dot is one of the 21 Dwaine cities (target population, disposable income). Tap the query point that is a hidden extrapolation: inside each variable's own range but outside the joint cloud.
why: Target population 75 is inside the observed 38 to 91 range, and income 16 is inside the observed 15.8 to 19.1 range, yet no real city pairs a population that large with income that low: the predictors are correlated (r = 0.78), so the cloud is a tilted ellipse. The query point sits in its empty lower-right corner. That is hidden extrapolation (Section 8.7), invisible to any one-variable range check.

prompt: One sentence about R-squared and adjusted R-squared is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: R-squared never falls when a predictor is added, even a column of pure noise.
Adjusted R-squared can fall when a predictor does not earn its degree of freedom.
For the savings model, adjusted R-squared (0.2797) is below R-squared (0.3385).
A higher in-sample R-squared always means better prediction on new data.

why: R-squared = 1 - SSE/SSTO can only rise as predictors are added, so it cannot tell a real gain from overfitting (Section 8.6). Adjusted R-squared charges a degrees-of-freedom rent through (n-1)/(n-p) and can fall, which is why it sits below R-squared here. A larger in-sample R-squared often reflects noise the model soaked up, and it can predict worse out of sample.
